meta.reconciliation_note: Backlog re-shaped 2026-05-28 from three inputs: external adversarial review (~/github/scratch/eval.md), internal repo-health 2026-05-28 (.work/reviews/repo-health-2026-05-28/report.md), and the existential alternatives.md draft (docs/alternatives.md). Backlog ordered top-down by Phase-1 priority. See PR chore/reconcile-phase-1 for rationale.

active.title: fix: .github/ files parsed by GitHub must be real files, not symlinks
active.description: GitHub does not follow symlinks for files it parses directly (workflows, dependabot, PR template, CODEOWNERS). install.py currently symlinks all these paths from skill/hedl/ projections, so the CI gate has never actually run in CI — all 'runs' since PR #2 are event:push fallback failures with zero check_runs created (verified via `gh api repos/:owner/:repo/commits/<sha>/check-runs` on multiple PR head SHAs: 218d3729, 452a2848, 5c6df42, 97b24a3). The UI label 'This run likely failed because of a workflow file issue' is GitHub rejecting the symlink-text as an invalid workflow file. This invalidates the 'PR-side CI gate' assumption that ADR-014 (Security/quality friction-aware) and the required-status-checks plan in WORK-0029 rest on. Discovered while investigating WORK-0029's acceptance-criterion-4 follow-up — the WORK-0029 investigation note's first hypothesis (no `required_status_checks` on main) is correct as written for the time, but is downstream of this root cause: even if `required_status_checks` had been configured, GitHub never had a context to attach because the workflow never published one.
acceptance_criteria: install.py distinguishes 'GitHub-parsed' .github paths from 'workflow-invoked' .github paths. For GitHub-parsed paths (workflows/*, dependabot.yml, PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md, CODEOWNERS), it COPIES the file content rather than symlinks.
install.py --doctor detects drift between the projected copy and the canonical source in skill/hedl/workflows/ (and equivalents) and fails with a clear remedy.
Existing symlinked .github files in this repo are replaced by real copies via install.py re-run (or a dedicated migration step).
After the fix, `gh api repos/neilgfoster/hedl/commits/<sha>/check-runs` shows real check_runs for the matrix names (am_i_done (3.11) through am_i_done (3.14)) on at least one PR head.
A corrected investigation note in .work/insights/WORK-0030-<topic>.md supersedes the WORK-0029 note's hypothesis with the actual root cause and links back to it.
am_i_done.py passes.
followups: After WORK-0030 lands and check_runs are confirmed via `gh api`, apply branch protection on main: required_status_checks with contexts ['am_i_done (3.11)', 'am_i_done (3.12)', 'am_i_done (3.13)', 'am_i_done (3.14)'] and strict: true.

backlog.title: fix: .hedl-tier ships without a hardcoded developer absolute path
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 (BLOCKING #1) — `.hedl-tier` is committed with `"skill": "/home/nfoster/github/scratch/hedl/skill/hedl"`. Any fresh clone is born broken because the path does not exist on a new operator's machine. Either (a) `.hedl-tier` must not be tracked at all and is generated by install.py at install time (preferred), or (b) the committed file stores a repo-relative path that install.py resolves. Whichever option is chosen must also update install.py, am_i_done.py, and any consumer that reads `.hedl-tier` to handle the new contract, and must add a test that a fresh clone + `install.py --tier <X>` produces a working state without manual edits.
acceptance_criteria: A fresh clone of the repo on a different machine can run `python3 skill/hedl/scripts/install.py --tier team` (and lightweight, gate) without manual edits and without hitting the absent-path error.
.hedl-tier either is gitignored and generated by install.py, or contains only a repo-relative skill path resolved at read time.
Test simulates fresh-clone state (no .hedl-tier, or .hedl-tier with relative path) and exercises the install + gate flow end-to-end.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: chore: author phase-0.json DoD retroactively and open phase-1.json
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 (BLOCKING #2) — `.work/phases/phase-0.json` is byte-identical to the EDIT-ME template; `.work/context.json` still says `phase_0_complete: false` despite seven merged Phase-1-scoped PRs. CLAUDE.md asserts a configured Setup phase that does not exist. Author phase-0.json with the real Setup goal/DoD/constraints that the merged PRs were actually executing against, run `/phase-complete` to transition properly (writing the retro), and open phase-1.json with a credible Phase-1 DoD anchored to the BLOCKING + governance items at the top of this backlog. Without this, Phase 1 has no defined exit criteria.
acceptance_criteria: .work/phases/phase-0.json contains real (non-EDIT-ME) goal, DoD, constraints, learning_goals, assumptions_to_validate.
.work/phases/phase-1.json exists with a credible DoD that maps to the BLOCKING and governance items at the top of this backlog.
.work/phases/phases.json transition_log records the phase-0 → phase-1 transition.
.work/context.json state.phase_0_complete = true and the EDIT-ME stub key is removed.
Transition was performed via /phase-complete (not by direct edits to phase files); retro committed under .work/reviews/.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: fix: startup.sh printf format-string injection (RCE)
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 (BLOCKING #6a, security-auditor) — `.claude/startup.sh` lines ~6-8 interpolate a project-derived name into a `printf` format string without sanitisation. A repo name containing printf conversion specifiers (`%s`, `%n`, etc.) controls the format string and can read/write attacker-chosen memory or arbitrary output. Replace the format-string interpolation with `printf '%s' "$name"` or equivalent.
acceptance_criteria: startup.sh treats all dynamic values as `%s` arguments, never as the format string itself.
Test or smoke-check confirms a repo dir name containing `%n` / `%s` produces literal output, not a format substitution.
shellcheck (or equivalent) passes on startup.sh.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: fix: install.py enforces SKILL_ROOT / repo containment on tier projections (RCE)
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 (BLOCKING #6b, security-auditor) — `install.py:258-273` applies `tiers.json` source/target paths without verifying containment in SKILL_ROOT (for source) or the consumer repo (for target). A malicious or accidentally crafted tiers.json entry with `..` or an absolute path can write outside the intended directories. Resolve every source/target to an absolute, real path and reject any path that escapes the expected root before performing the projection.
acceptance_criteria: install.py resolves and validates that every projection source lies inside SKILL_ROOT and every target lies inside the consumer repo before any filesystem write.
Containment check uses `Path.resolve(strict=False)` + prefix comparison (not string `startswith` on raw input).
Tests cover: relative escape (`../`), absolute path injection, and symlink-based escape attempts.
Behaviour on a well-formed tiers.json is unchanged.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: fix: am_i_done.py [verify] cmd allow-list / safe execution (RCE)
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 (BLOCKING #6c, security-auditor) — `am_i_done.py:169-203` runs arbitrary `[verify.<name>] cmd` strings from `hedl.toml` with no allow-list. Any PR that adds a `[verify.x] cmd = "…"` becomes an RCE on the next gate run (locally and in CI). Constrain execution: require `cmd` be a list (not a shell string), execute without `shell=True`, restrict the executable to a configured allow-list of binaries (e.g., `pytest`, `mypy`, `ruff`, `npm`, `pnpm`, `make`, plus an opt-in list from hedl.toml), and document the contract in docs/versioning.md and tiers.md.
acceptance_criteria: am_i_done.py rejects hedl.toml `[verify.<name>]` entries that use shell strings, shell metacharacters, or executables outside the allow-list.
subprocess invocation uses an argv list and `shell=False`.
Default allow-list is documented; operator extension path is documented and itself constrained (e.g., still no shell expansion).
Tests cover: shell metacharacter rejection, disallowed executable rejection, allow-list extension, and a happy-path verify invocation.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: fix: install.py guards .hedl-tier parsing and detects include cycles
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 (BLOCKING #7, edge-case-hunter) — `cmd_status` / `cmd_doctor` (install.py:303, ~397) call `json.loads(.hedl-tier)` with no try/except: a hand-edited or partial file crashes the CLI with a traceback instead of a useful message. Separately, `_flatten_projections` / `_flatten_inits` recurse via `includes` (install.py:99-105) with no cycle detection: a tiers.json cycle hangs or stack-overflows. Add a single corruption-aware reader for `.hedl-tier` (returning a structured error / hint) and add cycle detection to the `includes` flattening (visited-set, clear error naming the cycle).
acceptance_criteria: Malformed `.hedl-tier` (truncated JSON, missing keys, wrong types) produces an actionable error message — no Python traceback.
A cycle in tiers.json `includes` is detected, names the cycle path, and exits non-zero — no hang, no stack overflow.
Tests cover both malformed-marker and includes-cycle cases.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: fix: release.py next_version handles non-canonical --current-version
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 (BLOCKING #8, edge-case-hunter) — `release.py:94-95,136` `next_version()` raises IndexError/ValueError on non-canonical `--current-version` (e.g., `1.2`, `v1.2.3`, `1.2.3-rc1`), and the traceback ends up inside what callers expect to be machine-readable JSON output. Validate input against an explicit semver-ish regex, return a structured error response (non-zero exit, parseable error JSON) for non-canonical input, and document the supported version grammar.
acceptance_criteria: release.py rejects non-canonical --current-version with a parseable error (non-zero exit, JSON error envelope) instead of an unhandled exception.
Supported version grammar is documented adjacent to the script.
Tests cover representative invalid inputs (`1.2`, `v1.2.3`, `1.2.3-rc1`, empty string) and confirm canonical inputs still behave.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: chore: Phase 1 = discipline-restoration (tracker)
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 (BLOCKING #4 goal-displacement, #5 validation-theatre, existential-challenger) — ~4650 lines of process artefacts (ADRs, SKILL, agents, commands, references) vs ~4145 lines of Python + tests, and one shipped capability (the gate). The product had become the process. `.work/reviews/` and `.work/insights/` held only README/.gitkeep before the 2026-05-28 review run; the 'every agent output is validated' loop had never closed with a saved finding. This is a governance fact, not a single fixable defect. It is captured here as a tracker so the rest of Phase 1 (everything below) can be measured against it: BLOCKING fixes that produce running code, governance closures that bring ADRs into line with implementation, README that leads with the gate, and the workstream model that makes 'one shipped capability per workstream' the unit of progress. This item closes only when every other Phase-1 item closes.
acceptance_criteria: Every other Phase-1 item is closed before this one.

Repo-health re-run after Phase 1 close shows the goal-displacement and validation-theatre findings resolved (process-to-code ratio improved or the surplus process retired; reviews directory contains at least one real run produced inside Phase 1).
.work/reviews/ contains at least the 2026-05-28 run plus the Phase-1-close existential cycle.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: promote existential-challenger to a named agent and add to dispatch-rules
backlog.description: ADR-017 requires `existential-challenger` to be a mandatory reviewer for any `.work/decisions/*.md` write. Currently `existential-challenger` exists only as a prompt in `skill/hedl/references/review-library.md`; the gate's config check expects mandatory agents to be named agents in `.claude/agents/`. Repo-health 2026-05-28 reaffirms this: `dispatch-rules.json` maps `^\.work/decisions/` to `historian` only. Mirror the `determinism-auditor` promotion pattern: create `.claude/agents/existential-challenger.md` from the library prompt, remove the duplicate from review-library.md (library drops from 19 to 18 prompts), add `existential-challenger` to `.work/config/dispatch-rules.json` as mandatory for `.work/decisions/**` and `docs/alternatives.md` paths, and update all counts that reference '7 named agents' / '19 composable prompts' to '8' / '18' across README, tiers.md, docs/agents.md, and the generated SKILL.md section.
acceptance_criteria: `.claude/agents/existential-challenger.md` exists with valid frontmatter (mirror format of determinism-auditor).
The duplicate prompt is removed from review-library.md (library count drops to 18).
`existential-challenger` is in dispatch-rules.json as mandatory for `.work/decisions/**` and `docs/alternatives.md`.
All count references updated to '8 named agents' / '18 composable prompts' across README, tiers.md, docs/agents.md, generated SKILL.md.
am_i_done.py passes including the config check.

backlog.title: docs: update Principle 4 in CLAUDE.md from 'one work item at a time' to 'one per operator'
backlog.description: Per ADR-018, Principle 4 in CLAUDE.md (`One work item at a time — finish and validate before starting the next.`) is clarified as **one per operator**, not one per repo. Repo-health 2026-05-28 confirms the wording at CLAUDE.md:35 is unchanged. Update the wording and every reference doc that quotes the principle.
acceptance_criteria: CLAUDE.md Principle 4 reads 'One work item per operator at a time'.
Any other reference docs that quote the principle (SKILL.md, references/) are updated consistently.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: reconcile ADR-022 state_backend storage with implementation (hedl.toml vs .work/context.json)
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 — ADR-022 is Accepted and specifies that `state_backend` configuration lives in `hedl.toml`; the actual code, tests, installer, and docs still use `.work/context.json`. Either (a) implement the ADR — move state_backend reads/writes into hedl.toml with a one-shot migration from context.json — or (b) supersede ADR-022 with an ADR that records the decision to keep state_backend in context.json and explain why. Whichever path is chosen must converge ADRs, code, tests, docs, and installer behaviour on a single source of truth. This item is a precondition for the backend-adapter framework (WORK-0012) in Phase 2.
acceptance_criteria: Either the code reads state_backend from hedl.toml (with migration from context.json) and ADR-022 is unchanged, or a new superseding ADR is committed and ADR-022's status is marked Superseded.
Exactly one source of truth for state_backend across code, tests, installer, and docs.
If hedl.toml is chosen: install.py migrates existing context.json state_backend entries; tests cover the migration.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: refactor: reconcile .work/ and skill/hedl/work-state/ duplicated state
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 — `.work/config/` and `skill/hedl/work-state/config/` are byte-identical committed twins (same shape for `phases/`, `session.json`, `work.json`). No doc explains the relationship; editing one silently drifts. Decide which is the source of truth (the installable template lives in `skill/hedl/work-state/` and the live repo state lives in `.work/`, which is the most likely intent), then enforce that: either delete the duplicate, or generate one from the other at install time, and add a gate check that catches future drift. Document the relationship in docs/getting-started.md.
acceptance_criteria: Single source of truth chosen and documented: which directory is the installable template, which is live state, and how install.py relates them.
Duplicate files are eliminated (deleted, generated, or symlinked, per the chosen model).
Gate check exists for drift between any retained duplicates.
Tests cover the chosen relationship (fresh install reproduces .work/ from the template).
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: fix: tiers.json team-tier description matches actual projections
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 — `tiers.json:46` describes team tier as 'Lightweight + Claude Code hooks'; README, tiers.md, and team-tier.md describe team tier as 'Lightweight + GitHub Issues backend + parallel worktrees with gate-enforced merge'; `install.py --tier team` actually only projects six Claude Code files. Three sources, three different stories. Audit what team tier actually delivers today, align tiers.json's description to that reality, and align the prose docs to the same reality (or, if the docs describe the intended end state, mark those parts explicitly as 'planned' with the work-item that delivers them).
acceptance_criteria: tiers.json team description matches what `install.py --tier team` actually projects.
README, tiers.md, team-tier.md describe the same team tier or clearly mark forward-looking elements as planned (with a referenced work item).
Test or doctor check fails if tiers.json description diverges from actual projection counts/paths.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: docs: reconcile workstream double-definition (recursive container vs WS-PLAN/REQ/TECH/ARCH categories)
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 — `standards.md` and `iterate.md` define workstream as one of four fixed categories (WS-PLAN/WS-REQ/WS-TECH/WS-ARCH); ADR-019 redefines workstream as a recursive container subsuming phase and work-item. Both meanings are live in shipped files; the contradiction will confuse any reader. Pick one canonical meaning and rename the other. Recommended: keep ADR-019's recursive workstream as the canonical primitive, and rename the four-category taxonomy to something narrower (e.g., 'work-class' or 'workstream-class') across standards.md, iterate.md, work.json, and any code paths that read the WS-* prefix. Pair with WORK-0015 (terminology audit) if helpful, but resolve the double-definition first so the audit has a target.
acceptance_criteria: One canonical meaning of 'workstream' across all shipped docs and code.
The other meaning is renamed consistently and the change is reflected in standards.md, iterate.md, work.json schema, and any am_i_done.py / install.py logic that touches the WS-* prefix.
ADR-019 reads as the source of truth for the recursive primitive (or, if the four-category meaning wins, ADR-019 is superseded with rationale).
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: docs: fix count drift (6 vs 7 core agents; 15 vs 18 commands; panel of 8)
backlog.description: Repo-health 2026-05-28 — three count drifts shipped today. (1) review-library.md says 'Six core agents' (misses determinism-auditor); /repo-health spec says 'all 6 core agents' so a Full run silently under-panels. (2) `tiers.md:78` says 'all 15 command behaviors'; commands.md has 18. (3) ADR-016 references 'full panel of 8'; only 7 named agents exist (and WORK-0005 will bring this to 8 by adding existential-challenger). Authoritative counts must derive from the filesystem; documentation references that name a number must either reference the same source or be updated together. Coordinate with WORK-0005 so the 7→8 update lands once and consistently.
acceptance_criteria: review-library.md and /repo-health spec reference the correct number of core agents (7 today, 8 after WORK-0005).
tiers.md and any other docs that name a command count reference commands.md's actual count (18).
ADR-016's 'panel of 8' is reconciled with the post-WORK-0005 state.
A determinism / drift-detector test catches future count divergence (filesystem count vs docs).
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: docs: README and positioning updates (lead with gate; invisible qualifier; iteration-layer framing)
backlog.description: Update README and docs/getting-started.md to reflect the architectural story established in this ADR series and confirmed by the 2026-05-28 alternatives.md draft. Three threads: (a) lead with the gate (ADR-011, corroborated by the external review and the alternatives.md disqualifier story — the deterministic completion gate is the genuine differentiator), (b) Hedl-as-iteration-layer framing per ADR-022 (Hedl plugs into PM systems; doesn't replace them), (c) explicit 'Use Hedl if...' qualifier section per ADR-023 including the invisible-mode case. Honesty-over-marketing per ADR-010: no percentage-gain claims, no comparative superlatives. The alternatives.md stub already exists (committed in PR #10); this work uses it as the source for the disqualifier wording rather than re-creating it.
acceptance_criteria: README opens with the gate as the headline capability (ADR-011 lead-with-gate), citing alternatives.md as the disqualifier source.
README contains a 'Use Hedl if...' qualifier section including the invisible-mode case from ADR-023.
README and getting-started.md frame Hedl as an iteration layer above PM systems, not a replacement.
Cross-link from README to docs/alternatives.md and from getting-started to the disqualifier section.
No marketing claims (no percentages, no 'X% faster', no superlatives) survive review.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: docs: terminology audit — workstream replaces phase/work-item where ADR-019 specifies
backlog.description: Per ADR-019, the flat phase + work-item model collapses to a single recursive workstream primitive. Audit all references and bring them into alignment: CLAUDE.md, SKILL.md, skill/hedl/references/*.md, prior ADRs that mention 'phase' or 'work item' in the now-deprecated sense, docs/*. Add a glossary entry mapping old→new where helpful. Where 'phase' is genuinely useful as 'a top-level workstream of release scope', the term remains; where 'work item' is genuinely useful as 'a leaf workstream', the term remains; otherwise standardise on 'workstream'. Depends on WORK-0027 (resolve the double-definition first so this audit has a single target).
acceptance_criteria: All references to 'work item' in the deprecated singular sense are reviewed and either updated to 'workstream' or annotated with 'leaf workstream'.
All references to 'phase' in the deprecated sense are reviewed and either updated to 'top-level workstream' or left if the phase terminology is still preferable.
A glossary entry (likely in standards.md or a new terminology.md) maps the relationship for future readers.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: implement recursive workstream data model + schema migration
backlog.description: Per ADR-019, `.work/work.json` migrates from flat active/backlog/completed arrays to a recursive tree of workstreams. A leaf-level workstream = today's work item; a top-level workstream = today's phase. Bump `schema_version` and add a migration in install.py's registry that converts existing flat state to the recursive shape idempotently (with archival per the existing migration pattern). Solo + flat usage must remain functionally unchanged — leaf-only workstreams are the default for lightweight tier. Recursive composition is opt-in. Depends on WORK-0027 (canonical workstream definition) being settled.
acceptance_criteria: `.work/work.json` schema supports recursive workstreams (children: [workstream...]); flat work items remain valid as leaf workstreams.
`schema_version` increments; install.py --migrate converts existing flat state to recursive shape idempotently.
Pre-migration state is archived to `.work/archive/<timestamp>/` (mirroring existing archival pattern).
Per ADR-021 forward-compatibility: workstream IDs are globally unique enough to survive future cross-repo references (UUIDs or composite keys).
Lightweight-tier solo workflow is functionally unchanged.
am_i_done.py passes including all existing tests on the new shape.
Tests cover: leaf workstream (flat), nested workstreams (epic-of-stories), tier-graded behaviour, migration round-trip.

backlog.title: feat: ship existential-review and problem-statement workstream templates
backlog.description: Per ADR-020, two prose-form reference templates ship in `skill/hedl/templates/workstreams/`. existential-review: iteration 1 = identify capability + competitors; iterations 2..N = investigate each competitor; final iteration = report and culling decision (consumed by ADR-012's managed competitive lifecycle). problem-statement: iteration 1 = broad problem + objective; iterations 2..N = discovery, planning, refinement; final iteration = deliverables (may spawn child workstreams). Workstream creation flow consults the template at start; the agent follows the iteration pattern. Adopter-authored templates live alongside the shipped ones; path configurable via hedl.toml. Templates are prose-form for v0.2; executable templates deferred. The first concrete use of existential-review template is the workstream that produced the 2026-05-28 alternatives.md.
acceptance_criteria: `skill/hedl/templates/workstreams/existential-review.md` exists with the iteration pattern described above.
`skill/hedl/templates/workstreams/problem-statement.md` exists with the iteration pattern described above.
Workstream creation flow in references/commands.md describes consulting the template at start.
hedl.toml supports a `[workstreams] templates_path` override.

First concrete use: existential-review runs on Hedl itself, producing docs/alternatives.md (already committed in PR #10 — reference it as the worked example); problem-statement is used to bootstrap Lume.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: fix: install.py path resolves in consumer repos
backlog.description: SKILL.md routes install operations to `python3 skill/hedl/scripts/install.py`. In this (self-hosted) repo `skill/hedl/` is at the root so the path resolves. In a consumer repo, the skill is symlinked to `.claude/skills/hedl/` and `install.py` is NOT projected by any tier in `tiers.json`. An agent in an installed consumer repo following SKILL.md verbatim will hit 'file not found' for every install operation: --status, --doctor, --tier change, --migrate. Fix options (TBD at implementation time): (a) project `install.py` under one of the tiers, (b) re-route SKILL.md to the symlinked skill location, (c) ship a thin shim that resolves the skill path. This finding came from an external review with consumer context; a self-hosted /repo-health run cannot detect it because the failing condition does not exist in this repo.
acceptance_criteria: Install management instructions in SKILL.md resolve and execute successfully in an installed consumer repo (any tier).
A test simulates the consumer layout (skill symlinked to .claude/skills/hedl/) and exercises --status, --doctor, --tier change, and --migrate without 'file not found'.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: fix: budget_manager does not create .work/ in gate-only tier
backlog.description: skill/hedl/scripts/budget_manager.py unconditionally calls `path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)` at lines ~118 and ~150. Gate-only adopters have no `.work/` by design — they install `--tier gate` precisely because they do not want the phase-tracking machinery. A budget operation silently creating `.work/` violates the tier contract and may confuse adopters into believing their tier is mis-configured. Invisible to a self-hosted /repo-health because this repo is team-tier and `.work/` exists from install. Fix: budget operations must detect the tier and either skip work-tracking writes on gate-only, route to a different location, or no-op cleanly. The exact resolution is part of the implementation. (Cross-link: alternatives.md flags budget_manager as a culling-candidate; if the budget tier mechanism is retired during Phase 1 the gate-only path is automatically resolved.)
acceptance_criteria: Running any budget_manager.py operation in a gate-only installed repo does not create `.work/` (or any subdirectory of it).
Tier-aware behaviour is covered by a test that installs `--tier gate` into a temp dir, runs the relevant budget operations, and asserts `.work/` is absent.
Existing team-tier behaviour of budget_manager is unchanged.
am_i_done.py passes

backlog.title: fix: versioning.md schema_version claim matches actual code
backlog.description: docs/versioning.md (around lines 69-70 and 108-118) claims that Hedl reads `schema_version` from `hedl.toml` under `[hedl] schema_version`. The actual code: install.py reads `schema_version` only from `.work/context.json` (see install.py:47-85); am_i_done.py reads `hedl.toml` only for `[verify]` (see am_i_done.py:151-152, 170-196). Neither reads `[hedl] schema_version` from hedl.toml. Either implement the `hedl.toml` read (treat the doc as the spec) or correct the doc to describe what the code actually does. Discovered via external review; self-hosted /repo-health cannot spot this because there is no spec-vs-code consistency check in the gate.
acceptance_criteria: Documentation in docs/versioning.md matches code behaviour exactly — either the read is implemented or the doc is corrected.
If the read is implemented: install.py and/or am_i_done.py read `[hedl] schema_version` from hedl.toml with documented precedence relative to `.work/context.json`, covered by tests.
If the doc is corrected: `change_class` is `docs` instead of `fix`; the doc states clearly where schema_version is stored and read from.
am_i_done.py passes

backlog.title: docs: retrospective Prior-art audit of ADR-001 through ADR-016
backlog.description: ADR-017 requires every ADR to include a `Prior art` section that names overlapping standards/practices and (if overlap exists) justifies Hedl's version. ADR-001 through ADR-016 were written before ADR-017 and do not include this section. Audit each one and add a Prior art section: name the overlapping practice (or state none), answer the three justification questions where overlap exists. Several of the early ADRs apply standard patterns (e.g. ADR-002 tiered adoption, ADR-007 naming convention, ADR-009 opinionated defaults) and should explicitly acknowledge this — being honest about not inventing is the point. ADR-017 does not block on this audit; ADR-001..016 remain Accepted but become more defensible with annotation. Deferred to Phase 2 because it is parallel-able and not on the critical path of any Phase-1 BLOCKING or governance closure.
acceptance_criteria: Each of ADR-001 through ADR-016 includes a `Prior art` section that either names overlapping standards/practices or states that no relevant prior art exists.

For ADRs with overlap: the section answers (a) what is genuinely different about Hedl's version, (b) why the difference is worth the cost, (c) what would have to change for Hedl to delegate to the existing thing.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: lightweight-tier multi-operator support (assignees, claim/release, flock)
backlog.description: Per ADR-018, lightweight tier must support multi-operator coordination via local `.work/` mechanisms. Add an `assignee` field to work items, change `meta.active_item` to `meta.active_items` keyed by assignee, wrap `.work/work.json` writes with `flock` (mirror the budget_manager.py pattern), and add a claim/release lifecycle (open → claimed → in_progress → released → completed). Bump `schema_version` and add a migration. Solo adoption must be unchanged — assignee defaults to the current operator. Deferred to Phase 2 because it depends on the recursive workstream schema (WORK-0010) landing first.
acceptance_criteria: Work items carry an `assignee` field (defaults to current git user if unset).
`meta.active_items` keyed by assignee replaces singular `meta.active_item`; backward-compat read is supported.
Concurrent writes to `.work/work.json` are flock-serialised (mirroring budget_manager).
Claim/release lifecycle: `/claim <id>` and `/release <id>` flows defined in references/commands.md.
`schema_version` increments; install.py migration handles the upgrade idempotently with state archival.
Solo operator workflow is functionally unchanged (no new prompts, no extra steps).
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: audit and harden team-tier GitHub Issues multi-operator integration
backlog.description: Per ADR-018, team tier inherits multi-operator semantics from the chosen state backend (GitHub Issues today). Audit the existing github-issues backend in install.py / am_i_done.py: does Hedl read GitHub assignees correctly? How are concurrent claims handled? Does the gate detect file-level conflicts across PRs from different operators? Fix or document any gaps found.
acceptance_criteria: Audit report committed (e.g. as a /repo-health-style review).
GitHub assignees are read into Hedl's view of work-item state.
Two operators claiming the same issue is detected and surfaced.
File-level conflict across operator PRs is caught by `--streams` (verify with a test).
Gaps are either fixed or documented as separate backlog items with clear scope.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: tier-transition coordination-state migrations in install.py
backlog.description: Per ADR-018, tier transitions preserve coordination state. install.py migration registry must handle lightweight↔team transitions for assignees and claims: lightweight→team maps local assignees to GitHub Issue assignees; team→lightweight pulls GitHub assignees back to local. Both directions are idempotent and archive prior state before overwriting.
acceptance_criteria: install.py --tier <new> handles assignee/claim migration in both lightweight↔team directions.
Pre-migration state is archived to `.work/archive/<timestamp>/` (mirroring existing migration archival pattern).
Migration is idempotent: re-running --tier same is a no-op.
Tests cover both upgrade and downgrade paths with assignee state present.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: backend adapter framework for PM-system pluggability
backlog.description: Per ADR-022, formalise the backend adapter contract that the existing state_backend = local-file | github-issues switch implies. Define the contract (read workstreams, write workstream state, list/claim/release, status transitions, comments/journey, auth handshake). Refactor the existing local-file and github-issues code paths to implement the contract. Ship one additional adapter (Jira recommended; Linear acceptable) as a real second example, with a backend-contract test suite. install.py learns backend-specific projection (auth config, custom fields where needed). Depends on WORK-0024 (state_backend storage reconciliation) landing first.
acceptance_criteria: Backend adapter contract is documented in skill/hedl/references/ (likely a new backends.md).
Existing local-file and github-issues paths refactored to the contract; no behavioural regression.
A backend-contract test suite exists; both default adapters pass.
At least one additional adapter (Jira or Linear) ships and passes the contract suite.
hedl.toml [state_backend.<name>] supports adapter-specific configuration; install.py prompts for required fields.
am_i_done.py passes.

backlog.title: feat: install.py --invisible / --make-visible / --skill-from flags
backlog.description: Per ADR-023, implement Hedl's invisible mode. Three install.py additions. (1) --invisible <tier> flag: performs a normal tier install AND inserts a delimited Hedl block into .gitignore covering .work/, .hedl-tier, .github/ projections, .claude/ projections, hedl.toml. (2) --make-visible flag: removes the gitignore block (additive-only, never touches other rules); state of files on disk is unchanged. (3) --skill-from <path> flag: installs from a skill at an arbitrary path (e.g., ~/tools/hedl/skill/hedl/) so the operator's project doesn't need the skill source committed. The CI-gate-doesn't-run-in-invisible-mode tradeoff is surfaced in install output and docs.
acceptance_criteria: `install.py --invisible <tier>` installs the tier AND adds a delimited `# >>> hedl >>>` ... `# <<< hedl <<<` block to .gitignore covering all Hedl artifacts.
`install.py --make-visible` removes the block (additive-only management; never touches other .gitignore rules).
`install.py --skill-from <path>` allows installation from a skill at an arbitrary path; default remains the current behaviour.
Invisible-mode install output explicitly warns that team CI gate does not run on the operator's PRs.
Tests cover: invisible install, make-visible round-trip, gitignore block idempotency, skill-from with both absolute and relative paths.
am_i_done.py passes.

completed.title: fix: gate-on-main is currently failing (report.md schema + alternatives.md line-length)
completed.description: Confirmed 2026-05-28 by running `python3 .github/scripts/am_i_done.py` on a clean checkout of `main`: the gate FAILs with two violations introduced by recently merged PRs. (1) `.work/reviews/repo-health-2026-05-28/report.md` (committed in PR #8) does not match the `review-report` schema enforced by the gate — it is missing the required sections `## Dimension Scores`, `## Blocking Findings`, `## Significant Findings`, `## Minor Findings`, `## Next Actions`, plus the required patterns `log line`, `session type`, `review depth`, `commit SHA`. The report uses different section names instead (`## BLOCKING findings`, `## SIGNIFICANT themes`, etc.). (2) `docs/alternatives.md:183` (committed in PR #10) is 89 chars, exceeding the configured 80-char limit. Until this is fixed, no Phase-1 PR can land green. Either rewrite the report to satisfy the schema, relax the schema to match the prose-style report, or both — and reflow the single long line in alternatives.md. This is itself another instance of validation-theatre captured by WORK-0018: the gate was bypassed (or never re-run) on the merging of PR #8 and #10. Investigate why the gate let those merges land and tighten the merge protection.
acceptance_criteria: Fresh `git checkout main` + `python3 .github/scripts/am_i_done.py` exits 0 with all checks PASS.
Either report.md is restructured to satisfy the review-report schema, or the schema is updated to accept the actual report shape (with rationale committed).
alternatives.md:183 reflowed to <= 80 chars (or the global line-length config raised with rationale).
Investigation note committed to .work/insights/ explaining how PR #8 and PR #10 merged green despite producing a red main, and what (if any) merge-protection adjustment is needed.
am_i_done.py passes.
